perturbation.original_text: Section 3. No Obligation Of Disclosure. Neither Party shall have any obligation to disclose its Confidential Information to the other. Either Party may, at any time, cease providing its Confidential Information to the other Party, and may require the return of Confidential Information previously disclosed by written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 3. No Obligation Of Disclosure. Neither Party shall have any obligation to disclose its Company Secrets to the other. Either Party may, at any time, cease providing its Confidential Information to the other Party, and may require the return of Confidential Information previously disclosed by written notice.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Confidential Information' is changed to 'Company Secrets' in the first sentence of Section 3. While seemingly similar, 'Company Secrets' isn't defined. This creates ambiguity: Can a party refuse to disclose information that qualifies as 'Confidential Information' because it's choosing not to disclose 'Company Secrets'? This contradicts the later sentences referring to 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 3. No Obligation Of Disclosure. Neither Party shall have any obligation to disclose its Company Secrets to the other. Either Party may, at any time, cease providing its Confidential Information to the other Party, and may require the return of Confidential Information previously disclosed by written notice.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' and uses it consistently. The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Company Secrets' in the first sentence of Section 3, but continues to use 'Confidential Information' in subsequent sentences within the same section. Since 'Company Secrets' is not defined, it creates a contradiction. A party could argue that they don't need to disclose something that qualifies as Confidential Information, because they don't consider it a 'Company Secret'. The Contradicted Text is the same as the Changed Text, and the change causes in-text contradiction because it uses two different terms, but continues to refer to 'Confidential Information' in the rest of the section.

perturbation.original_text: Section 4. Disclosure Required By Law. If any applicable law, regulation or court order requires Recipient to disclose any of Owner's Confidential Information, Recipient shall promptly notify Owner in writing prior to making any such disclosure, in order to facilitate Owner's efforts to protect its Confidential Information. In such circumstances, Recipient shall cooperate with Page 12 of 34 <PAGE> Owner, at Owner's reasonable expense, in seeking and obtaining protection for Owner's Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 4. Disclosure Required By Law. If any applicable law, regulation or court order requires Recipient to disclose any of Owner's Data, Recipient shall promptly notify Owner in writing prior to making any such disclosure, in order to facilitate Owner's efforts to protect its Confidential Information. In such circumstances, Recipient shall cooperate with Page 12 of 34 <PAGE> Owner, at Owner's reasonable expense, in seeking and obtaining protection for Owner's Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Data' in the first sentence of Section 4 creates a contradiction. Does 'Data' encompass all information, or only a subset? If a law requires disclosure of 'Data', is the recipient obligated to inform the owner only if that data also qualifies as 'Confidential Information', as implied by the subsequent sentences? The latter sentences still refer to 'Confidential Information' causing a contradiction with the first sentence.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 4. Disclosure Required By Law. If any applicable law, regulation or court order requires Recipient to disclose any of Owner's Data, Recipient shall promptly notify Owner in writing prior to making any such disclosure, in order to facilitate Owner's efforts to protect its Confidential Information. In such circumstances, Recipient shall cooperate with Page 12 of 34 <PAGE> Owner, at Owner's reasonable expense, in seeking and obtaining protection for Owner's Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Data' in the first sentence of Section 4, while the subsequent sentences retain the term 'Confidential Information', creates a contradiction. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text. The question is whether Data and Confidential Information are the same thing. Because they are not defined as such, it's ambiguous whether the recipient has to notify the owner if non-confidential data is requested. This ambiguity represents a strong in-text contradiction because it creates conflicting obligations and uncertainty about the scope of the disclosure requirement and is an example of Misaligned Terminology.

perturbation.original_text: Section 5. Title. Owner shall retain all ownership rights in and to the Confidential Information it discloses to Recipient. No licenses or rights under any patent, trademark, copyright, trade secret or other intellectual property right shall be granted or implied under this Agreement. Neither Party shall be obligated under this Agreement to acquire from or provide to the other Party any service or product.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 5. Title. Owner shall retain all ownership rights in and to the Owner's Portfolio it discloses to Recipient. No licenses or rights under any patent, trademark, copyright, trade secret or other intellectual property right shall be granted or implied under this Agreement. Neither Party shall be obligated under this Agreement to acquire from or provide to the other Party any service or product.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Owner's Portfolio' in Section 5 creates a clear contradiction because 'Owner's Portfolio' is undefined. The contract no longer clearly states the owner retains rights to the 'Confidential Information' that was clearly defined in Section 1. It's unclear if "Owner's Portfolio" contains Confidential Information, or something completely different.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 5. Title. Owner shall retain all ownership rights in and to the Owner's Portfolio it discloses to Recipient. No licenses or rights under any patent, trademark, copyright, trade secret or other intellectual property right shall be granted or implied under this Agreement. Neither Party shall be obligated under this Agreement to acquire from or provide to the other Party any service or product.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Owner's Portfolio' in Section 5. This creates a contradiction because 'Owner's Portfolio' is undefined, as per the Perturbation Explanation. The contradicted text is identical to the changed text, reinforcing the contradiction as the contract now lacks a clear statement that the owner retains rights to the 'Confidential Information' which was clearly defined in Section 1, which is what this clause should have been referring to. This introduces ambiguity, directly breaking compatibility with the original intent and creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Section 6. Termination. Either Party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party. All obligations of confidentiality shall survive such termination. Upon termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall promptly return all of Owner's Confidential Information provided to it in tangible form, together with any and all copies and/or summaries, and shall destroy all of Owner's Confidential Information that is electronically stored; provided, however, that each Party's legal department may retain one copy of the Confidential Information in its file solely for the purpose of identifying information designated as "Confidential Information."
perturbation.changed_text: Section 6. Termination. Either Party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party. All obligations of discretion shall survive such termination. Upon termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall promptly return all of Owner's Confidential Information provided to it in tangible form, together with any and all copies and/or summaries, and shall destroy all of Owner's Confidential Information that is electronically stored; provided, however, that each Party's legal department may retain one copy of the Confidential Information in its file solely for the purpose of identifying information designated as "Confidential Information."
perturbation.explanation: In Section 6, 'confidentiality' is changed to 'discretion.' Although similar concepts, 'discretion' isn't defined in the contract. This introduces ambiguity: what obligations survive termination? Only those related to what could be interpreted as 'discretion,' or all obligations related to 'Confidential Information' as stated later in the same paragraph? This creates an in-text contradiction and allows for dispute of the obligations that survive the termination of this agreement
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 6. Termination. Either Party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party. All obligations of discretion shall survive such termination. Upon termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall promptly return all of Owner's Confidential Information provided to it in tangible form, together with any and all copies and/or summaries, and shall destroy all of Owner's Confidential Information that is electronically stored; provided, however, that each Party's legal department may retain one copy of the Confidential Information in its file solely for the purpose of identifying information designated as "Confidential Information."
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'confidentiality' with 'discretion.' This introduces ambiguity, as 'discretion' is not defined in the contract, while 'Confidential Information' is referenced later in the same section. This creates an in-text contradiction because it's unclear which obligations survive termination – those related to 'discretion' (undefined) or those related to 'Confidential Information.' The Changed Text contradicts the Contradicted Text because it uses two similar words without defining them, causing an in-text contradiction as per the Misaligned Terminology definition.
